result:
I keep thinking about the quiet hour just before sunrise, when the sky is still a bruised violet and the birds haven’t decided whether it’s safe to sing. There’s a hush that feels inherited, like the world itself is pausing to remember how it once sounded before we arrived with our engines and neon. I like to imagine that hush is the same hush Neanderthals heard, that it predates us, impartial and patient.  

Sometimes I sneak outside in that hour wearing whatever clothes were nearest the bed, barefoot because tying shoes would feel like building a wall between the soles of my feet and the planet’s slow pulse. The concrete is cold, secretly damp, and it reminds me I’m temporary—warm skin cooling by the second, heart drumming a countdown I never learned to read. The air smells of iron and wet leaves, a mineral exhale that makes me think of meteorites and dinosaur bones, of everything that has already had its moment and surrendered the stage.

I walk to the corner where the streetlamp flickers like it’s unsure whether to keep pretending it’s night. Moths orbit it in loose figure-eights, tiny astronomers circling their private sun. I wonder if they taste the light the way we taste oranges—if the photons feel like citrus on whatever they use for tongues. Science would scold me for giving insects longing, but science doesn’t have to stand here in mismatched socks, feeling the small ache of being only one species among millions, all of us improvising.

Across the road, Mrs. Gutierrez has already taped tomorrow’s headline inside her window: another war, another vote, another storm. The newsprint is damp at the edges, bleeding ink like a confession. I want to tell her that headlines dissolve the same way we do—slowly if you’re lucky, suddenly if you’re not—but she’s asleep behind the headline, dreaming perhaps of her grandchildren’s teeth flashing white as they chase fireflies that don’t exist on this continent anymore.

I keep walking. A stray cat falls in beside me, tail high, conversationally silent. We understand each other; we’re both hunting versions of home that won’t ask us to explain why we left the last one. At the crosswalk she pauses, considers the imaginary traffic, then ghosts away into a hedge. I feel briefly widowed by her absence, which is ridiculous; still, the feeling lingers like the afterimage of a camera flash.

By the time the sky begins its gradual bruise-to-rose alchemy, I’ve looped back to my own porch. The house is breathing softly behind me—pipes ticking, refrigerator humming a low C. I sit on the step and press my cold toes against the warm skin of my ankles. Somewhere inside, a phone will start screaming soon, dragging me to screens and schedules and the polite amnesia we call productivity. But right now the horizon is a thin blade of light, and I am a single unrecorded thought in the universe’s enormous dark diary. No barcode, no deadline, just the faint citrus taste of photons on an imaginary tongue.
